Leah's Guide to Visiting Japan

An amalgamation of recommended places, useful links, and travel tipsMar 25, 2026

Introduction

I lived in Nagoya for about two years and had the chance to travel throughout Japan, visiting many incredible places along the way. This guide is a list of destinations I’ve personally explored, along with highlights of things to do in each. Think of this as an overview of the Nagoya Nostalgia series I mentioned in my previous post.

In this post, I have recommendations for Hokkaido, Tokyo, Nagoya, Kyoto, Osaka, Nara, Kobe, Hiroshima, and Okinawa. The sections below provide a very brief overview of notable spots and activities, and each entry includes a link for further information.

I've been to all these places unless noted otherwise, and plan to share more detailed stories and insights in future posts. Since everything here is based on my own experiences, I also encourage you to do additional research to find what best fits your interests and travel style.

At the end of the post, I’ve included a few extra tips for traveling in Japan.

Hokkaido

  • Sapporo Beer Museum: Discover the rich history of Japanese beer, enjoy curated tastings, and experience local restaurants along the way!

  • Mt. Moiwa Ropeway: Take a cable car (or hike) up Mt. Moiwa and get a spectacular view of Sapporo from above.

  • Shiroi Koibito Park: Visit the sweets factory where Japan's famous Shiroi Koibito (White Lover) cookies are made.

  • Otaru: A quaint canal-side city brimming with charming shops and captivating museums.

Scenery outside Otaru Aquarium | iPhone14

Tokyo

  • Shibuya: Shopping and entertainment district with the famous Shibuya crossing. Close to Shinjuku and Akihabara.

  • Shinjuku: Another large shopping and entertainment district, famous for Japanese night life. Close to Shibuya and Akihabara.

  • Akihabara: Paradise for anime and video game lovers. Check out Radio Kaikan! Close to Shinjuku and Shibuya.

  • Asakusa: Historic part of Tokyo, famous for very popular temples such as Sensoji.

  • Odaiba: Popular man-made island with large shopping centers and The Statue of Liberty!

  • Ichigaya Fishing Center: Catch-and-release fishing of giant carp near Ichigaya Station.

  • Kichijoji: A hidden gem located slightly outside Tokyo. I recommend visiting Inokashira Park, where you can take in the natural beauty while row boating or pedaling a swan boat!

Famous Akihabara Radio Kaikan building | iPhone14

Day Trips from Tokyo

  • Hitachi Seaside Park*: Spacious park with a variety of seasonal flowers.

  • Kamakura*: Coastal town outside of Tokyo. Famous of a giant Buddha statue.

  • Lake Kawaguchiko*: Views of Mt. Fuji from a beautiful lake.

* Disclaimer : I haven't been to any of these places, but one day hopefully!

Nagoya

  • Osu Shopping Center: Large shopping district that spans several streets throughout.

  • Atsuta Jingu Shrine: One of Japan's most important shrines. Houses one of the three imperial regalia, the sacred sword Kusanagi.

  • Shiratori Garden: Tranquil Japanese garden with a traditional tea house.

  • Hisaya-Odori Park: Central Nagoya park with shopping and Mirai TV tower.

  • Port of Nagoya Public Aquarium: On of Japan's largest aquariums near the port of Nagoya!.

  • Nagoya Castle: Famous Japanese castle built in 1612 (rebuilt in 1959).

Many people believe Nagoya is boring. As someone who has lived there, I don't think Nagoya is boring. I absolutely love Nagoya. Check out nagoyaisnotboring.com!

Mirai TV tower and Oasis21 at sundown | iPhone14

Kyoto

  • Fushimi Inari: Famous shrine with 10,000+ red gates. Usually very crowded, but less crowded the higher you climb!

  • Kinkakuji (Golden Pavilion): Beautiful golden temple built on a beautiful pond.

  • Arashiyama Bamboo Forest: Scenic bamboo grove. Also usually very crowded.

  • Kiyomizudera: Famous and very beautiful temple.

Kinkakuji and me | iPhone14

Osaka

  • Osaka Castle: Osaka's castle! Quite similar to Nagoya's castle in my opinion.

  • Dotonbori: Street famous for food, shopping, and night life.

Osaka Castle | iPhone14

Kobe

  • Kobe Port Tower: An iconic waterfront landmark offering panoramic views of Kobe’s skyline and harbor.

  • Kobe Ropeway and Herb Gardens: A scenic cable car ride leading to lush gardens filled with seasonal flowers, herbs, and sweeping mountain views.

  • Kobe Harborland: A lively shopping and entertainment district by the sea, featuring restaurants, boutiques, and waterfront strolls.

  • Arima Onsen: One of Japan’s oldest hot spring towns, known for its relaxing mineral-rich baths and traditional atmosphere.

  • Sorakuen Garden: A serene Japanese landscape garden in central Kobe, featuring traditional ponds, teahouses, and seasonal flowers.

  • Himeji Castle: A bit outside Kobe, famed for its elegant white exterior and well-preserved feudal architecture.

Sun rays in Sorakuen Garden | iPhone14

Nara

  • Nara Park: Featuring deer, deer, and more deer!

There's more where this one came from... | iPhone14

Hiroshima

  • Hiroshima Peace Memorial Museum: An intense museum documenting the impact of the atomic bombing and promoting peace. Check out the Hiroshima Peace Park outside the museum.

  • Hondori Shopping Street: A street lined with shops, cafes, and entertainment options.

  • Miyajima Island: A picturesque island famous for the floating torii gate of Itsukushima Shrine and scenic hiking trails.

Itsukushima Shrine on Miyajima | iPhone14

Okinawa

  • Okinawa Churaumi Aquarium: A very big and very beautiful aquarium. A must see if going to Okinawa.

  • Snorkeling: The clearest and bluest ocean I've ever seen, perfect for snorkeling.

Pursuing a fish | iPhone14

Tips for transportation

Trains are the most convenient way to get around Japan. Here are some apps that can make riding both bullet trains and local subways/trains easier:

  • smartEx: This app lets you buy bullet train tickets and scan a QR code directly at the gate. It can be a bit finicky, but once it works, it saves a lot of hassle. Be persistent when logging in. For guaranteed seating, buy reserved seat tickets. Otherwise, you may have to stand, as trains can get busy. Without this app, you might need two tickets per person to ride the bullet train, which is doable but slightly tricky. Here’s a helpful guide: Shinkansen Tickets: How to Buy and Use

  • Welcome Suica Mobile: Suica is a rechargeable transit card that works for trains, subways, and buses. This app is the digital version, allowing you to scan your phone at ticket gates. I personally used a physical card, but this app is convenient if you prefer not to buy individual tickets. Note : I think you can only load money on this app after you get to Japan.

Tips for booking accommodations

I usually book hotels through Agoda, which often has great deals, especially if you plan early. In Japan, you can choose shared dormitories (your own bunk bed in a room with ~10 people) for a budget-friendly option, or private rooms at reasonable prices if traveling with a friend.

General tips

I also recommend checking apps like Klook or Agoda for local activities. They often list museums and experiences that aren’t widely advertised.

If you’re visiting very popular spots, especially in Tokyo, make reservations at least two weeks in advance to avoid disappointment.

That's all I have for now, happy planning!

今日は明日も頑張っている　ー　End.
